meta.purpose: Fast AI onboarding and navigation for the current system plus the approved routing-policy migration and manual internal client provisioning parity plan.

entry-points.EP-001: FastAPI runtime bootstrap and router assembly
entry-points.EP-007: Operational CLI for admin and config checks

M-001.NAME: backend-core
M-001.owns: settings, security, DB sessions, dependency injection, admin bootstrap, topology bootstrap

exports.fn-settings: Runtime configuration values and environment parsing

M-002.NAME: users
M-002.owns: email auth, Telegram auth, profile and admin user operations

M-003.NAME: vpn
M-003.owns: vpn clients, config generation, QR, topology-aware provisioning and legacy VPNServer compatibility

exports.type-VPNNode: Physical entry or exit VPN node
exports.type-VPNRoute: Logical path between entry and exit nodes

M-004.NAME: billing
M-004.owns: plans, subscriptions, payments and YooKassa integration

M-005.NAME: referrals
M-005.owns: referral codes, referral links and bonus accounting

M-006.NAME: admin-api
M-006.owns: dashboard stats, analytics, migration-safe routing summary and privileged system endpoints

M-007.NAME: routing
M-007.owns: split-tunneling, ipset coordination, tunnel health and custom route runtime

exports.fn-resolve-effective-target: Compatibility route decision wrapper that preserves the RU baseline

M-009.NAME: frontend-user
M-009.owns: login, register, dashboard, config, subscription, referrals and settings UI

M-010.NAME: frontend-admin
M-010.owns: admin login, dashboard, users, servers, plans, analytics, settings and future route-policy UI

M-011.NAME: telegram-bot
M-011.owns: Telegram command handling and backend API orchestration

M-012.NAME: deploy-surface
M-012.owns: installation, deploy scripts, runtime composition, proxying, cert generation, host-tool preflight, host-level awg0 peer sync watchers and deploy-time secret handling

M-013.NAME: route-policy-resolver
M-013.owns: deterministic routing decisions from domain, wildcard, DNS-bound IP, CIDR and fallback policy

exports.fn-resolve-route-decision: Returns route target and decision reason for a domain or IP

M-014.NAME: domain-rule-store
M-014.owns: policy rule persistence, normalization, priority ordering and activation state

M-015.NAME: dns-observer
M-015.owns: DNS resolution binding, TTL-aware caching and policy refresh triggers

exports.fn-refresh-domain-bindings: Refreshes domain-to-IP bindings under TTL constraints
exports.fn-expire-stale-bindings: Expires stale DNS-derived bindings and emits traceable expiry events

M-016.NAME: route-decision-api
M-016.owns: policy CRUD, decision inspection and operator-facing route observability

exports.fn-policy-rule-crud: Admin CRUD endpoints for domain and CIDR routing rules
exports.fn-policy-decision-explain: Inspection endpoint for effective route decisions and active DNS bindings

M-017.NAME: route-sync-runtime
M-017.owns: incremental sync of policy decisions into host-level route sets and refresh actions

exports.fn-apply-runtime-route-sync: Applies routing-set changes safely on the host
exports.fn-build-route-sync-plan: Calculates add/remove diffs before touching host ipsets

M-018.NAME: manual-access-policy
M-018.owns: resolution of internal operator-managed identities plus explicit complimentary access state for non-billable family or personal clients

exports.fn-resolve-internal-user: Finds or creates the canonical internal user identity for manual client issuance
exports.fn-ensure-complimentary-access: Creates or reuses explicit non-billable access state without entering payment-provider flows

M-019.NAME: manual-client-cli
M-019.owns: operator-triggered manual client issuance, config export and QR/output handling over backend provisioning logic

exports.fn-create-internal-client: Backend CLI entry that provisions or reuses an internal client and exports a working config
exports.fn-export-manual-client-config: Writes config or QR output after backend provisioning succeeds

CrossLinks.link: Users depend on config, auth and DB helpers.
VPN depends on settings, crypto and DB lifecycle.
VPN clients are keyed by user identity.
VPN provisioning already depends on routing runtime for policy consistency.
Billing uses core config and DB facilities.
Referrals use core config and DB facilities.
Admin endpoints query user state.
Admin endpoints read VPN topology and client state.
Admin endpoints read billing state.
Admin endpoints read referral state.
Admin analytics now count active domain and CIDR policy rules directly.
Admin analytics expose active DNS binding counts for policy visibility.
Routing uses core settings and DB/session surfaces.
Routing manager now delegates effective route decisions to the policy resolver.
Routing manager translates legacy custom routes into domain and CIDR policy rules.
Tasks use DB session lifecycle.
User frontend consumes auth and profile APIs.
User frontend consumes config and VPN status APIs.
User frontend consumes subscription and payment APIs.
User frontend consumes referral APIs.
Admin frontend consumes privileged APIs.
Bot mirrors auth-related flows.
Bot mirrors config retrieval flows.
Bot reflects subscription state.
Deploy surface shapes VPN runtime viability.
Deploy surface shapes host-level routing viability.
Route policy decisions must be consumable by routing runtime.
Resolver reads ordered domain and CIDR rules.
Resolver consumes DNS-bound address state.
Rule storage is managed through admin-facing APIs.
DNS bindings trigger runtime sync updates.
Decision API explains resolver outcomes.
Admin UI presents rule editing and decision visibility.
Runtime sync extends existing host-level routing manager behavior.
Manual access policy extends user identity handling with operator-managed internal users.
Complimentary internal access remains part of subscription and billing state rather than a hidden bypass.
Manual access policy must feed the normal VPN provisioning path.
CLI issuance must resolve internal identity and complimentary access before provisioning.
CLI issuance exports configs only after normal VPN provisioning succeeds.
Deploy wrapper becomes a thin shell around backend CLI instead of generating raw configs directly.

hotspots.H-001: Topology migration still carries legacy VPNServer compatibility constraints.
hotspots.H-002: Routing is host-coupled and changes can mutate live system state.
hotspots.H-003: Resolver precedence must stay deterministic under mixed domain, DNS and CIDR inputs.
hotspots.H-004: DNS TTL expiry and CDN rebinding can silently invalidate policy unless observed explicitly.
hotspots.H-005: Incremental route sync is safer than full refresh but easier to get wrong.
hotspots.H-006: Complimentary internal access must stay explicit or billing and analytics will silently drift.
hotspots.H-007: Manual client issuance is unsafe if shell scripts bypass backend topology and config generation logic.

agent-navigation.routing-migration-entry: For domain-aware routing work, open backend/app/routing/router.py, backend/app/routing/models.py, backend/app/routing/manager.py and backend/app/vpn/service.py before changing code.
agent-navigation.manual-client-entry: For manual client provisioning work, open deploy/create-client.sh, backend/app/cli.py, backend/app/users/service.py, backend/app/billing/service.py and backend/app/vpn/service.py before changing code.
